title: Robustness of Entanglement Manipulation for almost i.i.d. sources
abstract: We study the robustness of asymptotic entanglement manipulation beyond the exact i.i.d. regime, focusing on Mazzola--Sutter--Renner (MSR) almost i.i.d. sources, which allow a sublinear number of deviations from a tensor-power structure. For pure MSR sources along a bipartite reference state $|φ\rangle_{AB}$, we prove that the entanglement concentration rate is robust: every rate below the entropy of entanglement $S(φ_A)$ remains achievable. Moreover, this can be done by a single Schur--Weyl concentration protocol that is universal within the MSR class, depending only on the reference state and not on the particular source sequence. For mixed MSR sources along a reference state $ρ_{AB}$, we prove a source-dependent entanglement-distillation achievability result: every rate below the coherent information $I(A\rangle B)_ρ$ of the reference state is achievable, although the entanglement distillation protocol may depend on the particular MSR source sequence. For the reverse task of entanglement dilution, we prove a rate-robustness theorem: the asymptotic entanglement cost of any MSR target sequence along $ρ_{AB}$ is at most $E_F^\infty(ρ_{AB})$, the regularized entanglement of formation of the reference state. To establish these results, we prove structural and entropic properties of MSR almost i.i.d. sequences which may be useful in other information-theoretic settings. Thus, for the achievability statements considered here, MSR almost i.i.d. perturbations exhibit the same asymptotic behaviour as their i.i.d. reference states, despite allowing sublinear deviations from a tensor-power structure.
summary: This paper proves that key entanglement manipulation rates—such as concentration and dilution costs—are robust to small, structured deviations from perfect independence in quantum sources. By analyzing MSR almost i.i.d. states, the authors show that these non-ideal sources maintain the same asymptotic performance bounds as ideal i.i.d. sources.
main_problem: The study investigates how robust asymptotic entanglement manipulation (concentration and dilution) remains when quantum sources deviate from the ideal independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) regime.
main_result: For MSR almost i.i.d. sources, the achievable rates for both concentration and dilution are shown to be governed by the same asymptotic bounds as their perfectly i.i.d. reference states.
method: The analysis employs advanced information-theoretic tools, including spectral entropy rates, Schur–Weyl duality, and structural characterizations of MSR defect spaces.
model_or_system: The model involves bipartite quantum systems whose state sequences are described by Mazzola–Sutter–Renner (MSR) almost i.i.d. sources, allowing for controlled sublinear deviations from a tensor-power structure.
key_observables: Entanglement concentration rate ($S(\varphi_A)$), entanglement cost ($	ext{EC}(ho_{AB})$), and coherent information ($I(Aangle B)_ho$).
important_parameters: The defect size sequence $r_n$ must satisfy $r_n = o(n)$, ensuring the deviation from i.i.d. is sublinear.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the source structure belongs to the MSR class, which mathematically controls the nature of the deviations from perfect independence.
figures_summary: Not specified in the provided notes; the analysis relies on mathematical proofs and structural theorems rather than figure descriptions.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing structural and entropic rigidity properties for MSR sources (showing spectral entropy rates are preserved). It then applies these results sequentially to prove achievability bounds for entanglement concentration (pure states) and entanglement cost/dilution (mixed states).
why_interesting: It provides rigorous mathematical proofs that the fundamental capacity theorems of quantum information theory hold even when physical sources exhibit controlled, non-ideal correlations, which is crucial for realistic quantum communication protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
